steps.title: The Good Part
steps.body:
Weeks 1 and 2 were about clearing the ground. Diagnosing what's broken. Learning to repair the cracks.

Week 3 is different. This is the week where marriage stops being a project and starts being a pleasure.

Connection Week is about emotional intimacy, friendship, physical closeness, and the daily practices that make two people genuinely enjoy each other.

Not just tolerate. Not just coexist. Enjoy.

This week, you're not fixing anything. You're building something. You're creating the kind of connection that makes the hard weeks worth it.

Connection isn't a feeling you wait for.
It's a practice you choose.

Every single day.

questions.label:
On a scale of 1–10, how connected do you feel to your partner RIGHT NOW?

What would make that number go up by just ONE point this week? (Not ten points — one. Small, achievable.)

Think about emotional connection separately from physical connection. Are they at the same level? If not, which needs more attention?

steps.title: The Friendship First Principle
steps.body:
Here's what decades of marriage research keep showing: the couples who stay happiest are the ones who genuinely like each other.

Not just love. Like.

They're curious about each other. They turn toward each other's bids. They have inside jokes and shared references. They enjoy hanging out together — even when there's nothing to do.

Romance is a fire. Friendship is the fireplace. Without the fireplace, the fire just burns everything down.

This week, you're going to build friendship back into the center of your marriage. Not through grand gestures — through daily habits of attention and curiosity.

steps.question: Strong romantic relationships usually start with strong friendship and keep it at the center.
feedback: Correct. The best relationships are built on genuine friendship. Passion without friendship fades. Friendship plus passion is what lasts decades.
Actually, yes — research consistently shows that couples who maintain strong friendship remain happiest long-term. Friendship is the container that holds everything else.

questions.label:
Answer these questions honestly:

• When was the last time you laughed together — really laughed?
• When was the last time you had a conversation that wasn't about logistics, kids, or money?
• If your partner was a stranger, would you want to be friends with them?
• What do you know about their inner world right now — their worries, dreams, private thoughts?

The answers tell you where your friendship stands.

steps.title: The Love Map
steps.body:
Gottman calls it a 'Love Map' — your internal knowledge of your partner's world.

A strong love map means you know:
• Who's their closest friend right now, and why
• What's their biggest stressor this week
• What's a small win they had today
• What they'd want for their birthday, not what they told you to get
• What's making them anxious that they haven't mentioned

A weak love map means you know the broad strokes but not the details. You know they're stressed but not about what. You know they're happy but not why.

This week, you'll rebuild your love map — not by quizzing each other, but by staying curious.

steps.question: A 'Love Map' refers to:
options: A shared list of date night ideas
Your detailed knowledge of your partner's inner world
A map of places you've been together
feedback: Not quite. That's a date idea list — nice, but different.
Yes. A Love Map is your knowledge of their internal landscape — stresses, dreams, joys, fears. Strong love maps predict happy marriages.
Nope. Though those can be fun memories!
Close, but a Love Map is broader — it covers everything in their inner world.

steps.title: Two Questions Today
steps.prompt:
Ask your partner two questions today that aren't about logistics:

1. 'What was the best part of your day?'
2. 'What's something you're looking forward to?'

Listen without interrupting. Ask one follow-up question. That's it.

The goal isn't a deep conversation. The goal is the habit of turning toward each other.
steps.body:
Ask your partner two questions today that aren't about logistics:

1. 'What was the best part of your day?'
2. 'What's something you're looking forward to?'

Listen without interrupting. Ask one follow-up question. That's it.

The goal isn't a deep conversation. The goal is the habit of turning toward each other.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Emotional Intimacy — Going Deeper
steps.body: Today was about re-establishing friendship and curiosity. Tomorrow, we go deeper — into the vulnerability and emotional intimacy that transforms a good friendship into a great marriage.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
